skill.name: lipsync
skill.description: Route lip-sync jobs through RunComfy CLI models for source videos, portrait avatars, and script-driven speaking videos.
skill.summary: Create lip-synced videos with RunComfy models from video, portrait, audio, or script inputs.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns. Review confirms the command and network activity are expected RunComfy CLI behavior, while weak-crypto and reconnaissance hits are false positives from prose and model names. The remaining concern is medium risk because the skill sends user media to external RunComfy services and supports dual-use lip-sync workflows.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Invocation for Media Processing
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to invoke the RunComfy CLI with user-selected model routes and media inputs. This is expected behavior, but it runs external commands and should remain limited to the documented runcomfy command surface.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly grants Bash access for runcomfy and documents multiple runcomfy run invocations. The context shows legitimate CLI usage, not arbitrary shell execution.

medium_findings.title: External Network Processing of User Media
medium_findings.description: The workflow sends video, image, and audio URLs to RunComfy model endpoints and downloads generated outputs. This is necessary for the skill, but users should know that media is processed by an external service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation states that the CLI posts to the Model API, polls status, and downloads RunComfy URLs. The endpoints are allowlisted in the file, so this is expected network use rather than hidden exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Lip-Sync Capability Without Technical Enforcement
medium_findings.description: The skill can create synthetic speech-aligned faces and states that consent checks rest with the operator. This is a real abuse risk even though the file includes policy guidance and no malicious prompt injection was found.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly describes dual-use risks and says the skill itself does not gate inputs. The concern is semantic misuse risk, not hidden malicious code.

low_findings.title: Documented Token Storage in User Config
low_findings.description: The static filesystem finding points to documentation that runcomfy login stores a token under the user config directory with mode 0600. The skill does not include code that reads arbitrary hidden files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path is explicitly documented and relates to normal CLI authentication. It is still worth surfacing because API tokens are sensitive credentials.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported blocker patterns occur in descriptive text, route names, tips, and an exit-code table. No evidence found of cryptographic operations, system probing, or reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited areas shows prose and documentation, not crypto APIs or host inspection commands. The static labels do not match the surrounding context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Permission for RunComfy CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access scoped to runcomfy commands. This is a controlled command surface, but it still executes an external CLI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and scope statement are explicit. The risk is bounded to runcomfy commands rather than general shell access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Model API and Output Downloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI posts requests to RunComfy model APIs and downloads generated files from RunComfy domains. Users should avoid sending media without rights or consent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicitly described and endpoint-limited. It is not hidden, but it involves third-party media processing.

content.value_statement: Producing lip-synced video requires choosing the right model for each input type. This skill routes video, portrait, audio, and script workflows through the RunComfy CLI.
actual_capabilities: Chooses a RunComfy lip-sync route based on the available inputs.
Supports source video plus audio workflows with Sync Labs, Kling, or Creatify.
Supports portrait plus audio workflows with OmniHuman.
Supports script-only generation through models that create speech in the same pass.
Provides RunComfy CLI invocation patterns and output directory guidance.
Includes consent, voice provenance, and endpoint allowlist guidance.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and a valid RunComfy account or token.
Processes media through external RunComfy services.
Does not technically enforce consent or identity rights checks.
Model quality depends on source media, audio clarity, and duration matching.

use_cases.title: Dub Product Videos
use_cases.description: Create localized versions of approved brand videos with translated voiceover audio.

use_cases.title: Generate Presenter Clips
use_cases.description: Turn a consented portrait and voiceover into a talking presenter video.

use_cases.title: Prototype Multilingual Campaigns
use_cases.description: Test several languages or scripts before committing to final video production.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Video Dub
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner source video plus voiceover workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Lip sync this approved source video to this voiceover audio. Use the best default RunComfy route for quality.

prompt_templates.title: Portrait Presenter
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a talking presenter video from this consented portrait and this clean voiceover. Use the avatar-style route.

prompt_templates.title: Cost-Aware Batch Run
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a cost-aware lip-sync plan for these approved videos and voiceovers. Prefer standard quality unless hero quality is needed.

prompt_templates.title: Route Comparison
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced model selection and tradeoff analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the RunComfy lip-sync routes for this project. Recommend the model, input format, quality tier, and consent checks.

output_examples.input: A brand video and a translated Spanish voiceover are provided.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends Sync Labs sync v2 Pro for a high-quality dub and describes the needed RunComfy inputs.

output_examples.input: A creator portrait and a product pitch audio file are provided.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends OmniHuman for an avatar-style presenter and notes portrait framing and consent requirements.

output_examples.input: Only a short advertising script is available.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a text-to-video lip-sync route that can generate speech and video together.

best_practices: Confirm consent for the face, voice, and source media before running a job.
Use clean speech audio and match audio length to the video duration.
Use the documented RunComfy endpoints and avoid arbitrary install scripts.
anti_patterns: Do not target real people, public figures, or private individuals without consent.
Do not send confidential media to external services without approval.
Do not use script-only routes when exact pre-recorded voice sync is required.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps create lip-synced videos through RunComfy CLI models for video, portrait, audio, and script workflows.

faq.question: Does it require RunComfy?
faq.answer: Yes. It depends on the RunComfy CLI and a RunComfy account or token.

faq.question: Can it use an existing video?
faq.answer: Yes. It can route source video plus audio jobs to Sync Labs, Kling, or Creatify models.

faq.question: Can it make a portrait speak?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends avatar-style routes such as OmniHuman when the input is a portrait and audio.

faq.question: Is it safe for public figures?
faq.answer: Only with clear consent and rights. The skill warns operators to refuse non-consensual or harmful requests.

faq.question: Where is media processed?
faq.answer: Media is processed by RunComfy services through the CLI and documented RunComfy model endpoints.
